text:
Underwater image restoration via multiscale optical attenuation 
compensation and adaptive dark channel dehazing
Shuai Liu a,b, Peng Chen a,b, Jianyu Lan c, Jianru Li d, Zhengxiang Shen a,b,*, 
Zhanshan Wang a,b,*
a School of Physical Science and Engineering, Tongji University, Shanghai, 200092, China
b Institute of Precision Optical Engineering, Tongji University, Shanghai, 200092, China
c State Key laboratory of Space Power-Sourecs, Shanghai Institute of Space Power-Sources, Shanghai, 200245, China
d School of Ocean and Earth Science, Tongji University, Shanghai, 200092, China
A R T I C L E  I N F O
Keywords:
Underwater image restoration
Color correction
Dark channel prior
Underwater image dehazing
A B S T R A C T
Underwater images often suffer from color cast and low visibility due to inherent factors such as 
light absorption, scattering, and turbidity. The quality-degraded underwater images are unfa­
vorable for underwater research and applications.To effectively deal with these quality degra­
dation issues, this paper presents a novel restoration framework tailored specifically for 
underwater images, aiming to restore their natural clarity and improve their visual quality. 
Firstly, a multi-scale optical attenuation compensation color correction algorithm is employed to 
correct the color deviations of underwater images. Subsequently, an adaptive dark channel 
dehazing algorithm is proposed, including the global background light estimation algorithm 
based on multiple optical prior properties and a more sensitive segmentation transmission map 
estimation algorithm. Our approach integrates advanced image restoration techniques with 
domain-specific optimizations, ensuring robust performance across diverse underwater condi­
tions. We comprehensively evaluate our method on a wide range of underwater image datasets, 
demonstrating its effectiveness in restoring color fidelity, contrast, and texture details. Further­
more, we analyze the quantitative and qualitative impacts of our framework, showcasing its 
advantages over existing state-of-the-art methods. Our work not only advances the field of un­
derwater image restoration but also provides valuable insights into designing future restoration 
algorithms for this domain.
1. Introduction
Underwater imaging systems play an essential part in various marine research fields, including marine military fields, marine 
environmental protection, and marine engineering construction [1–3], laying an important foundation for human exploration of the 
mysterious ocean depths. However

framework, showcasing its 
advantages over existing state-of-the-art methods. Our work not only advances the field of un­
derwater image restoration but also provides valuable insights into designing future restoration 
algorithms for this domain.
1. Introduction
Underwater imaging systems play an essential part in various marine research fields, including marine military fields, marine 
environmental protection, and marine engineering construction [1–3], laying an important foundation for human exploration of the 
mysterious ocean depths. However, underwater imaging technology has long been a technical challenge restricting underwater 
observation and engineering operations. Remotely Operated Vehicles (ROVs) play a crucial role in underwater observation and en­
gineering operations, such as underwater wet insertions and extractions. The operation of ROVs relies primarily on engineers 
monitoring video transmitted from underwater cameras. Therefore, high video clarity is essential for precise maneuvering. Poor video

quality can not only significantly prolong operation time but also pose severe safety risks, potentially jeopardizing the successful 
execution of tasks. Therefore, providing clear underwater images is a crucial essential for the efficient and safe operation of ROVs 
during underwater construction and subsequent maintenance phases. Additionally, the clarity of underwater image data transmitted 
by ROVs to laboratories is a key factor influencing the successful conduct of scientific research. However, due to the absorption and 
scattering of light in water, images captured by these systems often suffer from severe color distortions [4–6], detail blurring, and 
insufficient contrast [7]. The degradation of underwater image quality imposes significant limitations on visual perception analysis 
and practical underwater applications. Consequently, developing an effective underwater image restoration method is of great 
importance for subsea construction, maintenance, and subsequent scientific research [8–10].
To address the challenging issues of severe color distortions, detail blurring and insufficient contrast, various underwater image 
processing works have been proposed. Generally, these methods can be roughly summarized as image restoration [11–13], image 
enhancement [14–16], and deep learning methods [17–20]. Specifically, image restoration relies on various prior knowledge to es­
timate unknown parameters and subsequently inverts the underwater imaging model to recover clear images. Unfortunately, most 
existing image restoration methods overlook the diverse spectral properties of water, resulting in suboptimal image quality. Unlike 
image restoration, image enhancement methods do not require prior knowledge. They improve the contrast and brightness of un­
derwater images by directly adjusting pixel values. However, these methods often neglect image texture details, leading to issues such 
as color distortion, noise amplification, and oversaturation in the enhanced images. Deep learning methods require a large number of 
high-quality paired images for model training. However, obtaining high-quality, large-scale and paired underwater images is chal­
lenging, which limits the performance of deep learning approaches. Additionally, the highly dynamic and complex underwater 
environment can further contribute to the instability of deep learning methods.
In our work, we propose an underwater imaging system using multiscale optical attenuation compensation and adaptive dark 
channel dehazing. The system enhances visual quality by compensating for the limitations inherent in underwater imaging systems. In 
the first stage, we first extract RGB channel information from the image obtained by the camera. Then, we perform multiscale optical 
attenuation compensation and propose a minimal color loss function to adjust the naturalness of the image

can further contribute to the instability of deep learning methods.
In our work, we propose an underwater imaging system using multiscale optical attenuation compensation and adaptive dark 
channel dehazing. The system enhances visual quality by compensating for the limitations inherent in underwater imaging systems. In 
the first stage, we first extract RGB channel information from the image obtained by the camera. Then, we perform multiscale optical 
attenuation compensation and propose a minimal color loss function to adjust the naturalness of the image colors. In the second stage, 
we propose an adaptive dehazing algorithm on the basis of the dark channel. The proposed method estimates the background light and 
the transmission rate on the basis of the optical characteristics for underwater imaging, effectively improving image visibility and 
contrast. Through both qualitative and quantitative analyses, the system demonstrates superior color naturalness and contrast, 
ensuring adaptability across a diverse range of marine environments.
2. Related works
Currently, researchers have proposed various methods to improve underwater visual perception, which can be broadly categorized 
into three main categories: image restoration, image enhancement, and deep learning methods. The overview of these methods is 
provided as follows.
Image restoration methods utilize priors or assumptions to estimate the transmittance and backlighting of the underwater scenery. 
Subsequently, these methods perform an inversion on the optical forming model to acquire better visual and clearer underwater re­
covery images. A representative restoration method is the Dark Channel Prior (DCP) proposed by He et al. [11] in 2011. Although DCP 
is effective in most cases for dehazing, its effectiveness on underwater images is limited due to its neglect of the light absorption effects 
of the underwater medium. The literature [12] proposed the underwater dark channel prior (UDCP) to achieve better underwater 
results. To overcome these limitations, literature [13] used a systematic underwater image enhancement algorithm that combines an 
underwater image dehazing algorithm and a contrast enhancement algorithm. Nevertheless, these methods often fail to fully account 
for color shifts caused by light attenuation, resulting in images with blurred details and textures.
Image enhancement methods improve image quality by adjusting pixel values. For example, histogram equalization methods, 
retinex-based methods, and image fusion are commonly used in this category. Rahman et al. [14] developed the Multiscale Retinex 
with Color Restoration (MSRCR) algorithm, which integrates color stability with partial comparison and brightness enhancement. 
Zhuang et al. [15

for color shifts caused by light attenuation, resulting in images with blurred details and textures.
Image enhancement methods improve image quality by adjusting pixel values. For example, histogram equalization methods, 
retinex-based methods, and image fusion are commonly used in this category. Rahman et al. [14] developed the Multiscale Retinex 
with Color Restoration (MSRCR) algorithm, which integrates color stability with partial comparison and brightness enhancement. 
Zhuang et al. [15] proposed a Bayesian correction method using multi-gradient prior reflectance and lighting for single underwater 
images. Then, in the literature [16], a retinex variational model inspired by a hyper-laplacian reflection prior was proposed to improve 
the visual quality of underwater images, and some results were achieved. However, these methods often overlook the texture details of 
the image, resulting in oversaturation issues in the enhanced images.
Deep learning methods aim to automatically extract representation features from training data and establish a nonlinear mapping 
relationship, which includes primarily Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN) and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN). Jiang et al. 
[17] designed a Lightweight Cascaded Network (LCNet) based on Laplacian image pyramids to enhance underwater images’ visual 
quality. Zhang et al. [18] proposed a novel network called Cascaded Network with Multi-level Sub-networks (CNMS), which selec­
tively cascaded multiple sub-networks through triple attention modules to extract distinct features from underwater images, bolstering 
the network’s robustness and improving generalization capabilities. Liu et al. [19] designed a Weak-Strong Dual Supervised Gener­
ative Adversarial Network (WSDS-GAN) for UIE, which enhanced image brightness and corrected image color in the first weakly 
supervised learning phase and enhanced image texture details in the second strongly supervised learning phase. However, these 
deep-learning methods require extensive data and prolonged training periods. Additionally, most of the data used in these methods is 
artificially synthesized, which differs from real-world underwater images, thus limiting the robustness and generalization capabilities 
of these methods [20].

3. Proposed method
The proposed underwater imaging system comprises two primary components: underwater image color correction and adaptive 
dark channel dehazing enhancement. The flowchart of the proposed system is shown in Fig. 1. Initially, images to be restored are 
captured using a camera operating platform. Then the multi-scale attenuation compensation and adaptive dark channel dehazing of 
the captured image are carried out. The processing result is displayed in the control system.
3.1. Multiscale optical attenuation compensation
Unlike standard images, underwater images are often afflicted with significant color casts on account of the unique underwater 
imaging and lighting conditions. This is because the absorption of light intensity by water grows exponentially and there is a significant 
difference in spectral absorption. Typically, the wavelength is proportional to the rate of attenuation, with longer wavelengths of red 
color disappearing at a depth of about 5 m, followed by brown and yellow light, with green and blue light persisting until depths of 30 
to 60 m. As a result, acquired underwater images are prone to exhibit blue-green characteristics visually. Correcting these color de­
viations is crucial to obtaining high-visibility underwater images.
Different from previous color correction methods [22], we refer to reference [21] and propose a multi-scale optical attenuation 
compensation method: firstly, the three channels of the image are compensated at multiple scales, and then the minimum color loss 
function is designed for an iterative operation to obtain underwater images with more natural colors. Initially, we perform channel 
redefinition according to the average value of each color channel of the original image. The average value of each color channel of the 
original image can be respectively expressed as:

images on land are similar. Since the large color channel has the least amount of loss, to increase its dynamic range, we use the linear

function is calculated according to the adjusted three channels. If the loss is greater than 10-2, redefine the large, medium and small

satisfy the minimum loss function, the color channels are further stretched using Eq. (2) to obtain the final color image Iʹ

3.2. Adaptive dark channel dehazing
The visual effect degradation of underwater images is similar to that of foggy images, primarily because of absorption and scat­
tering of light by the underwater environment used for propagation. Therefore, at this stage, researchers mostly use the extended 
atmospheric scattering model for optical imaging analysis of underwater images [24]. The extended atmospheric scattering model can 
be shown as follow: 
Ic(x) = Jc(x)tc(x) + Ac(x)(1 −tc(x)), c ∈{r, g, b}
(6) 
where xrepresents every pixel point in the image, Ic(x) is the raw image captured, Jc(x) is the clear image after processing, Ac(x) is the 
global background light, and tc(x) ∈[0, 1] is the transmission map which indicates the proportion of the location radiance reaching the 
camera. The goal of image dehazing is to obtain Jc(x) using imaging model inversion by estimating Ac(x) and tc(x) from Ic(x). To 
address this difficult uncertainty problem, we propose an effective adaptive dehazing algorithm that comprises two primary steps: 
global background light estimation and transmission map estimation. 
1) Global background light estimation
According to previous studies, the brightest pixel point in the underwater image is usually selected as the global background light 
Ac(x). Nevertheless, variations in lighting conditions and the presence of white objects or noise can violate this assumption. Therefore, 
in order to reliably estimate the global background light, image brightness is initially utilized as a criterion for

, we propose an effective adaptive dehazing algorithm that comprises two primary steps: 
global background light estimation and transmission map estimation.

The region obtained by two hierarchical searches is used as the background light candidate region. To mitigate the error of

point corresponding to the same position of the original image at the position of the 0.1% highest pixel point in the dark channel map is 
defined as the candidate pixel point for the background light (based on the a priori information of the dark channel

8) 
The region obtained by two hierarchical searches is used as the background light candidate region. To mitigate the error of 
background light selection by suspended particles, the dark channel map of the background light candidate area is computed. The pixel 
point corresponding to the same position of the original image at the position of the 0.1% highest pixel point in the dark channel map is 
defined as the candidate pixel point for the background light (based on the a priori information of the dark channel). According to the 
characteristics of underwater optical imaging, the longer wavelengths of red attenuate faster than green and blue, meaning red light 
typically spreads over the shortest distance in water. Whereas the background light is at a location away from the camera, the farther 
away from the camera, the greater the difference in intensity between channels with varying attenuation rates. Therefore, when the 
mean value of the blue channel of the input graphic is greater than the mean value of the green channel, the pixel point with the largest 
blue-red channel ratio is searched among the candidate pixel points as the global background light. When the mean value of the green 
channel of the image is higher than the mean value of the blue channel, the pixel with the largest green-red channel ratio is searched as 
the global background light among the candidate pixel points. This operation effectively reduces interference from overly bright 
targets in the water. The above calculation method can largely avoid the interference of external factors and improve the accuracy and 
robustness of global background light estimation. 
2) Transmission map estimation:
The estimation of a transmission map usually relies on a variety of assumptions, priors, or models. For example, the underwater 
Dark Channel Prior (UDCP) estimates the transmission map based solely on blue and green channels, because red channels tend to 
decay rapidly. However, light attenuation in water varies depending on the water type, and red light decays rapidly only in the open 
ocean and cannot be used to estimate transmission. Therefore, we propose an adaptive dark channel estimation algorithm for 
transmission maps. First, the average value of the red channel is compared with the value of the threshold σ. According to experience, 
the threshold σ is set to 56 in this paper. If the mean value of the red channel is less than σ, the transmission map tc(x) is expressed as: 
tc(x) = tgb(x) = 1 −miny∈Ω(x)

The estimated transmittance map is then refined using a guided image filter to remove blocky artifacts caused by image block 
estimation, with the original image as the guide. In the end, we can get the recovered clear underwater image by Eq. (6).
3.3. Camera system operation
When we use the self-developed underwater camera for underwater photography, the captured underwater scene image can be 
processed with the proposed algorithm through the operation display interface. The enhanced image will appear on the display. And 
we can also control the underwater camera by operating other programs in the display interface, which is shown in Fig. 3.
4. Experiments and discussions
In this section, we show a large number of experimental results including the comparative conclusions of the color correction effect 
performed on UCCS [26], the comparative results of the dehazing effect performed on the UIEB dataset [30], and the demonstration of 
the experimental results in the field under three different scenes. A great amount of experiments prove that the proposed method has a 
significant contribution to the visual quality recovery of underwater images. All experiments were conducted on a PC with an Intel 
Core i7-13700k CPU (3.40 GHz), 64 GB RAM, and the 64-bit Windows 10 operating system, using Matlab R2022a software. We analyze 
the proposed algorithm in terms of color correction effect, adaptive dehazing and practical application capability.
4.1. Underwater image color correction evaluation
To accurately evaluate the color correction capability of the proposed algorithms, we tested its performance on the UCCS dataset 
[26]. UCCS is a subset of the large-scale Real-world Underwater Image Enhancement (RUIE) data set and contains three 100-images 
subsets of bluish, blue-green, and greenish tones. The subset is designed to assess the effectiveness of enhancement algorithms in 
removing color cast. We compared our method with Gray World, White-patch [27], Shade-of-Gray [28], and Gray-Edge [29]. The 
visual comparison of the calibration results obtained through each algorithm is shown in Fig. 4. A key observation is that the proposed 
method achieves color correction that more closely resemble real-world terrestrial images, ensuring a more natural and

blue-green, and greenish tones. The subset is designed to assess the effectiveness of enhancement algorithms in 
removing color cast. We compared our method with Gray World, White-patch [27], Shade-of-Gray [28], and Gray-Edge [29]. The 
visual comparison of the calibration results obtained through each algorithm is shown in Fig. 4. A key observation is that the proposed 
method achieves color correction that more closely resemble real-world terrestrial images, ensuring a more natural and perceptually 
accurate enhancement. Specifically, Gray World introduces an undesired red bias. White-Patch fails to enhance bluish images effec­
tively, though it provides slight improvements for blue-green and green images. Shade-of-Gray introduces a red bias in both blue-green 
and green images, leading to noticeable color distortions. In contrast, Gray-Edge has a minimal impact on color cast correction, failing 
to effectively neutralize the unwanted color shifts. In comparison, our method effectively balances color naturalness without over­
saturation and surpasses the other approaches in contrast enhancement and detail preservation. And the processing results of our 
method are closer to the ground real images. To further validate the effectiveness of our method quantitatively, we analyzed RGB 
histograms of the images in Fig. 4, as shown in Fig. 5. The histograms illustrate the distribution of pixel intensities across the three color 
channels, providing a quantitative measure of color balance. Compared to other methods, the proposed algorithm exhibits a more 
balanced distribution across the RGB channels, with peak positions and intensity trends closely aligning with the ground truth. This 
indicates that our method effectively reduces color cast while preserving the natural color distribution.
4.2. Underwater image adaptive dehazing evaluation
To evaluate the robustness and effectiveness of the proposed method in adaptive dehazing, we conducted experiments on several

underwater images from the UIEB dataset [30]. The UIEB dataset contains of 950 real-world underwater images, including 890 with 
corresponding reference images and 60 challenging datas without references. CLUIE-Net [10], DCP [11], UDCP [12], MSRCR [14], L1 
[15], HLRP [16], PFMD [20] and TIP [21] are applied as comparative approaches. Firstly, different images were randomly selected 
from raw 890 images in the UIEB dataset for visualization, as shown in Fig. 5. The DCP method exhibits limited effectiveness on raw 
underwater images as it fails to account for selective color attenuation in underwater environments. Although the UDCP method can 
remove haze, it tends to produce darker images with noticeable color distortion. The MSRCR algorithm is unstable and often produces 
images with excessive darkening effects. The L1 method suffers from structural ambiguity and unnatural color, whereas the HLRP 
method tends to over-enhance images, particularly evident in the first comparison image in Fig. 6. Although the TIP method restores 
underwater images’ color and structures, it often introduces excessive red cast in the restored images. Furthermore, to comprehen­
sively evaluate the performance of deep learning-based methods, we additionally compared our approach with CLUIE-Net and RFMD. 
The CLUIE-Net method demonstrates significant shortcomings in color correction and dehazing effects when processing underwater 
images with blue and green tones, failing to effectively restore the color balance and clarity of the images. Particularly, when pro­
cessing the green-toned image in the sixth row, CLUIE-Net exhibits severe color distortion, leading to the loss of true color information 
in the images, which adversely affects visual quality and the accuracy of subsequent analysis. RFMD method also underperforms 
overall with its effects in color correction, dehazing and detail restoration falling short of expectations, indicating its limited adapt­
ability in complex underwater environments. In comparison, our method surpasses other approaches in enhancing edge structures and 
texture details, restoring naturalness of images’ color, promoting contrast, and suppressing artifacts, demonstrating its effectiveness 
across diverse underwater conditions.
To further evaluate the advantages of our method, we applied it to 60 challenging underwater images. Fig. 7 presents the enhanced 
results obtained using different algorithms on six representative underwater images. As shown in Fig. 7

indicating its limited adapt­
ability in complex underwater environments. In comparison, our method surpasses other approaches in enhancing edge structures and 
texture details, restoring naturalness of images’ color, promoting contrast, and suppressing artifacts, demonstrating its effectiveness 
across diverse underwater conditions.
To further evaluate the advantages of our method, we applied it to 60 challenging underwater images. Fig. 7 presents the enhanced 
results obtained using different algorithms on six representative underwater images. As shown in Fig. 7(b-d), the DCP, UDCP and 
MSRCR methods fail to effectively eliminate haze and often introduce color cast, especially under severe color bias conditions. The L1 
method demonstrates satisfactory performance in color correction; however, it still suffers from image hazing issues, which limit its 
overall effectiveness in underwater image enhancement. The HLRP method achieves commendable performance in color correction 
and contrast enhancement; however, it is prone to overexposure issues. The TIP algorithm tends to introduce a red color cast, which 
may compromise the color fidelity of the enhanced images. CLUIE-Net enhances brightness to some extent and improves color dis­
tribution. However, the overall appearance remains dark with some images exhibiting a noticeable green or blue tint, failing to fully 
correct the color cast in underwater environments. The RFMD method produces images with a predominant yellow-green hue, 
indicating potential color bias in its color correction process. Additionally, its ability to preserve details is relatively weak and some 
images still exhibit significant haze, revealing limitations in dehazing capability. In contrast, the proposed algorithm achieves the best 
performance in color restoration, not only naturally recovering the original color of objects and reducing color cast in underwater 
images but also excelling in contrast and sharpness enhancement.
The performance of different methods was quantitatively analyzed based on four evaluation metrics: UIQM [31], UCIQE [32], PCQI 
[33] and Entropy, using 450 underwater images from the UIEB dataset. UIQM consists of a weighted combination of UISM (the un­
derwater image sharpness measure), UIConM (underwater image contrast measure), and UICM (underwater image colorfulness 
measure). UIQM quantifies three key visual attributes of underwater images: chroma, sharpness, and contrast. A higher UIQM value 
indicates a better balance among color, sharpness, and contrast. UCIQE is computed as a weighted sum of chroma, saturation

UIQM consists of a weighted combination of UISM (the un­
derwater image sharpness measure), UIConM (underwater image contrast measure), and UICM (underwater image colorfulness 
measure). UIQM quantifies three key visual attributes of underwater images: chroma, sharpness, and contrast. A higher UIQM value 
indicates a better balance among color, sharpness, and contrast. UCIQE is computed as a weighted sum of chroma, saturation, and 
contrast, providing a measure of nonuniform color cast, blurriness, and low contrast. A higher UCIQE value indicates that the image 
has a good trade-off between chroma, saturation, and contrast. PCQI measures perceptual distortions by comparing enhanced and raw 
images in terms of mean intensity, signal strength, and structural fidelity. The higher the PCQI value, the better the contrast quality. 
Entropy quantifies the amount of information in an image; a higher Entropy value indicates greater information content.
The quantitative evaluation results of different methods on the UIEB dataset are presented in Table 1. The proposed method 
achieves the highest scores in UIQM (4.1383), UCIQE (0.6908), and PCQI (1.2038), indicating its superior performance in terms of 
color restoration, contrast, and structural fidelity. Notably, Entropy measures the amount of information retained in the image, the 
proposed method ranks third (7.3734), slightly lower than the L1 algorithm (7.6376) and CLUIE-Net (7.3894). This suggests that while 
the proposed method effectively enhances image quality, it maintains a competitive level of information richness. Overall, compared to 
the competitive techniques evaluated, the proposed method demonstrates superior performance, highlighting its robustness and 
effectiveness.

turbidity, we used the system to capture images of a cup under different turbidity conditions in the laboratory water tank. The 
experimental scene is shown in Fig. 8, where Fig. 8(a) is the state of clean water, Fig. 8(b) represents the condition of turbid water with 
deep sea sediment, and the red box indicates the turbidimeter to measure the turbidity of the pool.
We process the pictures of cups with different turbidity, and the recovery effect is shown in Fig. 9. The first row of Fig. 9 shows the 
original images of turbidity from 2 NTU to 13 NTU. It can be seen that the images become increasingly blurred until they are almost 
completely unviewable. The second row of Fig. 9 displays the processed images using the proposed algorithm. In Figs 9(a) and 9(b), the 
restored images are relatively clear, with vivid colors and high contrast. In Figs 9(c) and (d), although the captured images are quite 
turbid, the method still effectively restores the cup images. Even in Figs. 9(f), where the captured image is nearly unviewable, the 
method can still recover the general colors and contours of the cup.
At the Shanghai Salvage Bureau, we conducted field experiments and the experimental scenario is shown in Fig. 10, where the 
underwater camera is marked with a blue box. We captured scenes commonly encountered during salvage operations and processed 
the images using the system, the recovery effect is shown in Fig. 11. Despite the typically high turbidity of these scenes, our system 
performed exceptionally well in processing the images. The processed images showed significantly improved visibility, with previously 
blurry details becoming clearer and overall contrast markedly enhanced. These recovery effects demonstrate that the proposed system 
can effectively improve image quality even in high-turbidity environments, expanding its application range and showcasing its po­
tential for practical use in salvage operations.
Additionally, we conducted underwater experiments in the South China Sea, as shown in Fig. 12. We captured some raw images 
that generally exhibited color bias, blurriness, and low contrast. Then we processed these raw images with our designed system. The 
results clearly show a significant improvement in image clarity and color restoration. Previously blurry details became much clearer, 
and the overall contrast was noticeably enhanced. The recovery effects indicate that the proposed system can effectively enhance the

practical use in salvage operations.
Additionally, we conducted underwater experiments in the South China Sea, as shown in Fig. 12. We captured some raw images 
that generally exhibited color bias, blurriness, and low contrast. Then we processed these raw images with our designed system. The 
results clearly show a significant improvement in image clarity and color restoration. Previously blurry details became much clearer, 
and the overall contrast was noticeably enhanced. The recovery effects indicate that the proposed system can effectively enhance the 
visual effect of underwater images. The processed images are significantly better than the raw ones, demonstrating not only the 
system’s effectiveness but also its potential for practical applications in underwater environments.
The system has been tested in the high-turbidity water environment at the Shanghai Salvage Bureau, and the experimental results 
show that it can significantly enhance the clarity of underwater images and the visibility of targets, laying the foundation for sub­
sequent application in actual salvage operations. In addition, the experiments conducted in the South China Sea further validated the 
system’s effectiveness in complex underwater environments with significant variations in lighting and depth, proving its capability to 
correct color deviations, enhance image details and improve low contrast. These experiments not only demonstrate the system’s 
reliability in specific scenarios but also highlight its potential for broader underwater applications, such as marine biological research, 
seabed mapping, and deep-sea archaeology. High-quality underwater images are crucial for scientific research and engineering ap­
plications. To meet the real-time visual processing needs of ROVs, we will further optimize the algorithm’s computational efficiency to 
support near-real-time processing. As a result, the system holds great potential for integration into ROVs and autonomous underwater 
vehicles (AUVs), enhancing their ability to achieve more precise target recognition and operational support in complex aquatic en­
vironments. This integration would offer strong technological support for advancing human exploration and utilization of the ocean.

4.5. Ablation study
To illustrate the effectiveness of each component of our method, we perform the following ablation studies on the UCCS [26], UIQS 
[26], and EUVP [36] datasets, including: only the multiscale optical attenuation compensation in Section 3.1 (-oMOAC), only the 
adaptive dark channel dehazing in Section 3.2 (-oADCD) and our proposed method. Their restored results are presented in Fig. 13. The 
results indicate that -oMOAC effectively corrects color distortion, leading to a more natural color balance. However, it exhibits limited 
contrast and suboptimal dehazing performance, resulting in residual haze in the restored images. In contrast, -oADCD significantly 
Fig. 13. Ablation study of the proposed method, line 1,2: the datas in UCCS, line 3,4: the datas in UIQS, line 5,6: the datas in EUVP.
S. Liu et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                              
Computers and Electrical Engineering 123 (2025) 110228 
15

improves image contrast and demonstrates a strong dehazing capability, yet its color correction performance is relatively poor, leading 
to noticeable color cast in the restored images. By integrating both components, our proposed method achieves a well-balanced 
enhancement that effectively mitigates color distortion, improves contrast and removes haze, resulting in visually superior under­
water images.
Table 3 reports the UIQM [31], UCIQE [32], PCQI [33] and Entropy scores for each core module of our method tested on the UCCS 
[26], UIQS [26], and EUVP [36] datasets. As shown in Table 3, the proposed method integrates both components, leading to a more 
balanced enhancement. It consistently demonstrates superior overall performance, effectively improving color fidelity, contrast, and 
perceptual quality in underwater images.
5. Conclusion
This paper presents a special underwater imaging system based on a self-developed underwater camera. Its core lies in the software 
part of image recovery based on the underwater optical attenuation a priori. This system carefully considered the complexities of 
underwater light decay, striving to obtain a natural color image through the minimum loss function. The adaptive dark channel al­
gorithm can increase the contrast and sharpness of the image effectively, and make the restored image have richer details. Experi­
mental results highlighted the system’s capability to produce high-quality images, enhancing their visual appeal. The system can 
reveal more details and valuable information. Although the algorithm we designed has shown better performance in images from 
different scenes, it is not perfect and without limitations. Our method has limited in real-time image processing. It requires further 
improvement in computational efficiency in future work.
